3 min read
5 hours ago

--

Building a modern web application requires more than creating an attractive user interface. A reliable backend must securely manage data, authenticate users, and provide consistent communication between the client and database.
For my WTWR (What To Wear?) project, I developed a full REST API that allows users to create accounts, manage clothing items, and interact with personalized weather-based recommendations. This project challenged me to move beyond frontend development and understand how authentication, authorization, databases, and server-side logic work together to create a complete application.
Building the Foundation of the API
I started this project by creating a backend server using Node.js and Express. The goal was to build an API capable of handling requests from the frontend application and communicating with a MongoDB database.
Using Express routing, I organized endpoints for users and clothing items. This structure allowed the application to separate responsibilities and keep the code easier to maintain.
Designing Database Models with MongoDB and Mongoose
One of the important parts of this project was designing schemas that accurately represented the application’s data.
Using Mongoose, I created models for users and clothing items. Each clothing item stores information such as:

• Item name

• Weather category

• Image URL

• Owner reference

• User likes
I also connected clothing items to their owners using MongoDB ObjectId references. This relationship allowed the API to determine which user created each item and control access to protected actions.
Implementing Authentication and Authorization
A major focus of this sprint was adding authentication and authorization.
I implemented user registration and login functionality using JSON Web Tokens (JWT). After successfully logging in, users receive a token that identifies them when making requests to protected routes.
Authorization ensures users can only modify their own data. For example, a user can delete only clothing items they created, preventing unauthorized changes to another user’s information.
Building an API also requires handling unexpected situations. I created centralized error responses for common problems such as invalid IDs, missing resources, validation failures, and server errors.
Instead of returning default Express error pages, the API provides consistent JSON responses that the frontend can properly process.
One of the biggest challenges during development was understanding how different parts of a backend system connect together. Debugging authentication issues, database relationships, and API responses helped me develop a stronger understanding of server-side development.
Coming from an operations management background, I approached debugging the same way I approach solving workplace problems: identifying the issue, breaking it into smaller pieces, testing solutions, and improving the process.
Creating the WTWR API helped me expand my skills from frontend development into full-stack engineering. I successfully built a backend application with authentication, authorization, database management, and secure API routes.
This project reinforced the importance of writing organized code, designing reliable systems, and considering security from the beginning.
Moving forward, I would continue improving this project by adding additional features such as expanded user profiles, improved recommendation logic, and additional testing coverage.
This project represents an important step in my journey toward becoming a software engineer, and I look forward to continuing to build and share new applications.
